payload.summary: Missing bounds check on JPEG SOS segment length field causes out-of-bounds array access in cbs_jpeg_split_fragment
payload.mechanism: In function cbs_jpeg_split_fragment, when encountering a JPEG_MARKER_SOS marker, the code reads a 16-bit length value from the input fragment via AV_RB16(frag->data + start) but proceeds to use this attacker-controlled length without validating that it does not exceed the available data range (end - start). When a crafted input provides a length value larger than the remaining data, subsequent processing that relies on this length reads beyond the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds array access. The fix inserts an early length-vs-bounds check: if (length > end - start) return AVERROR_INVALIDDATA;

payload.pattern: if (length > end - start)
if (length > end - start)
if (length > end - start)

payload.description: In cbs_jpeg_split_fragment, the SOS marker segment length parsed via AV_RB16(frag->data + start) is used without any validation that it fits within the available data range (end - start).

An early bounds check is added: if (length > end - start) return AVERROR_INVALIDDATA; before the SOS segment data is processed further, preventing any out-of-bounds access when length exceeds available data.
Guard condition if (length > end - start) return AVERROR_INVALIDDATA; rejects malformed input where the SOS segment length exceeds the remaining data size before any buffer access occurs.

Prior to the fix, no bounds check existed between the point where length = AV_RB16(frag->data + start) reads a 16-bit value from input data and the subsequent code that processes the SOS segment using that length.

payload.summary: Missing bounds check on JPEG SOS segment length field causes out-of-bounds array access in cbs_jpeg_split_fragment
payload.mechanism: In function cbs_jpeg_split_fragment, when encountering a JPEG_MARKER_SOS marker, the code reads a 16-bit length value from the input fragment via AV_RB16(frag->data + start) but proceeds to use this attacker-controlled length without validating that it does not exceed the available data range (end - start). When a crafted input provides a length value larger than the remaining data, subsequent processing that relies on this length reads beyond the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds array access. The fix inserts an early length-vs-bounds check: if (length > end - start) return AVERROR_INVALIDDATA;

errors: no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']
no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']

payload.summary: Use-after-free: `pls->segments[0]->url` passed directly to `av_probe_input_buffer` while the IO read callback may modify or free the segment URL during reload.
payload.mechanism: In `hls_read_header`, the string pointer `pls->segments[0]->url` is passed directly to `av_probe_input_buffer`. The commit message states "The segments / url can be modified by the io read when reloading." This means the URL buffer owned by the segment data structure can be modified or freed asynchronously by the IO read callback while `av_probe_input_buffer` still holds a reference, creating a use-after-free condition.

gate_errors: no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']
errors: no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']

payload.summary: Missing minimum-value guard: segment `duration` is assigned from parsed EXTINF without enforcing a minimum value, allowing zero or near-zero durations to reach segment scheduling logic.
payload.mechanism: In `parse_playlist`, the segment `duration` parsed from the EXTINF tag was assigned to `seg->duration` without any lower-bound check. A crafted playlist containing a duration of zero or a value below `0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE` could cause downstream division or scheduling operations to operate on near-zero duration values. The fix relocates the assignment after a guard that floors the duration to `0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE` (1ms).

gate_errors: no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']
errors: no declared fix set has complete gated CodeAnchor coverage: ['CVE-2020-13904:fix-set:1']

payload.description: The URL string pointer `pls->segments[0]->url` is passed directly to `av_probe_input_buffer(&pls->pb, &in_fmt, pls->segments[0]->url, NULL, 0, 0)` without a local copy. Per the commit message, the segment URL can be modified by the IO read callback during reload, causing use-after-free on the URL string.

The segment `duration` parsed from the EXTINF tag is assigned to `seg->duration` without any minimum-value check, allowing zero or near-zero duration values to be stored and used in downstream scheduling.

The URL is duplicated with `url = av_strdup(pls->segments[0]->url)`, the copy is passed to `av_probe_input_buffer`, and freed with `av_free(url)` after the call, ensuring the IO read callback cannot modify the URL string while it is in use.

A guard `if (duration < 0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE)` is added before the `seg->duration = duration` assignment, flooring the duration to `0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE` (1ms) when the parsed value is below that threshold.

payload.summary: Use-after-free: `pls->segments[0]->url` passed directly to `av_probe_input_buffer` while the IO read callback may modify or free the segment URL during reload.
payload.mechanism: In `hls_read_header`, the string pointer `pls->segments[0]->url` is passed directly to `av_probe_input_buffer`. The commit message states "The segments / url can be modified by the io read when reloading." This means the URL buffer owned by the segment data structure can be modified or freed asynchronously by the IO read callback while `av_probe_input_buffer` still holds a reference, creating a use-after-free condition.

payload.summary: Missing minimum-value guard: segment `duration` is assigned from parsed EXTINF without enforcing a minimum value, allowing zero or near-zero durations to reach segment scheduling logic.
payload.mechanism: In `parse_playlist`, the segment `duration` parsed from the EXTINF tag was assigned to `seg->duration` without any lower-bound check. A crafted playlist containing a duration of zero or a value below `0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE` could cause downstream division or scheduling operations to operate on near-zero duration values. The fix relocates the assignment after a guard that floors the duration to `0.001 * AV_TIME_BASE` (1ms).

payload.summary: Missing bounds validation on attacker-controlled operand index read from model file leads to out-of-bounds array write in ff_dnn_load_model_native
payload.mechanism: In ff_dnn_load_model_native(), the operand_index value is read from an untrusted model file via avio_rl32() and used directly as an array index into network->operands[] without first validating that operand_index < network->operands_num. An attacker can supply a malformed model file containing an operand_index exceeding the allocated operand array, causing an out-of-bounds memory write at oprd = &network->operands[operand_index] and subsequent writes through oprd->name and oprd->data_type, matching the CWE-787 out-of-bounds write classification.

payload.pattern: operand_index >= network->operands_num
operand_index >= network->operands_num

payload.description: operand_index read from untrusted model file via avio_rl32() is used directly as array index into network->operands[] at line 212 (old) without validating operand_index < network->operands_num
A bounds check if (operand_index >= network->operands_num) { goto fail; } is inserted before the oprd = &network->operands[operand_index] dereference to reject out-of-range operand indices
Guard condition operand_index >= network->operands_num validates that the file-supplied operand index does not exceed the allocated operand array before dereference

payload.summary: Missing bounds validation on attacker-controlled operand index read from model file leads to out-of-bounds array write in ff_dnn_load_model_native
payload.mechanism: In ff_dnn_load_model_native(), the operand_index value is read from an untrusted model file via avio_rl32() and used directly as an array index into network->operands[] without first validating that operand_index < network->operands_num. An attacker can supply a malformed model file containing an operand_index exceeding the allocated operand array, causing an out-of-bounds memory write at oprd = &network->operands[operand_index] and subsequent writes through oprd->name and oprd->data_type, matching the CWE-787 out-of-bounds write classification.

payload.summary: HEIC image dimension correction and SetImageExtent gated behind decode_options != NULL, causing mismatched image dimensions when decode_options is NULL
payload.mechanism: In ReadHEICImageByID, the code that corrects image->columns and image->rows from the decoded heif_image (via heif_image_get_width/height) and calls SetImageExtent to resize the image buffer was conditionally executed inside 'if (decode_options != (struct heif_decoding_options *) NULL)'. When decode_options is NULL, the image retains dimensions from heif_image_handle_get_width/height set earlier in the function, which may not match the dimensions of the decoded heif_image. The fix moves the dimension correction and SetImageExtent outside the conditional so they always execute, while keeping only heif_decoding_options_free inside the guard.

payload.pattern: image->columns=(size_t) heif_image_get_width(heif_image,heif_channel_Y); image->rows=(size_t) heif_image_get_height(heif_image,heif_channel_Y); status=SetImageExtent(image,image->columns,image->rows,exception);

payload.description: The width/height correction (heif_image_get_width/height from the decoded image) and the SetImageExtent call to resize the image buffer are conditionally executed inside 'if (decode_options != (struct heif_decoding_options *) NULL)', causing them to be skipped when decode_options is NULL.
The width/height correction and SetImageExtent are executed unconditionally after the decode entry check; only heif_decoding_options_free remains inside the decode_options != NULL guard.

The guard 'if (decode_options != (struct heif_decoding_options *) NULL)' incorrectly gates both the mandatory image dimension correction and the decode_options cleanup; the guard should only protect heif_decoding_options_free.

payload.summary: HEIC image dimension correction and SetImageExtent gated behind decode_options != NULL, causing mismatched image dimensions when decode_options is NULL
payload.mechanism: In ReadHEICImageByID, the code that corrects image->columns and image->rows from the decoded heif_image (via heif_image_get_width/height) and calls SetImageExtent to resize the image buffer was conditionally executed inside 'if (decode_options != (struct heif_decoding_options *) NULL)'. When decode_options is NULL, the image retains dimensions from heif_image_handle_get_width/height set earlier in the function, which may not match the dimensions of the decoded heif_image. The fix moves the dimension correction and SetImageExtent outside the conditional so they always execute, while keeping only heif_decoding_options_free inside the guard.

payload.summary: Use-after-free in SetImageAlphaChannel: ConformPixelInfo is called after GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels, and its internal cache operations can invalidate the acquired row pointer q before it is used in FlattenPixelInfo.
payload.mechanism: In the RemoveAlphaChannel path of SetImageAlphaChannel, GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels acquires a pointer q to a row in the image pixel cache. In the vulnerable ordering, ConformPixelInfo is subsequently called to conform the background pixel. Internally, ConformPixelInfo can trigger pixel cache reallocation or invalidation on the same image (e.g., through AcquireVirtualPixels or related cache-acquisition paths that may displace the existing cache view). This renders q a dangling pointer. The later call to FlattenPixelInfo then dereferences q, constituting a use-after-free (CWE-416). The fix reorders the two calls so that ConformPixelInfo executes before GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels, guaranteeing that any cache-modifying side effects are resolved before the row pointer is acquired.

payload.pattern: ConformPixelInfo(image,&image->background_color,&background,exception);
ConformPixelInfo(image,&image->background_color,&background,exception);

payload.description: In the RemoveAlphaChannel case of SetImageAlphaChannel, ConformPixelInfo is called after GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels has returned row pointer q, and ConformPixelInfo can internally modify the image pixel cache (e.g., via AcquireVirtualPixels or related allocation), making q a dangling pointer before it is later dereferenced in FlattenPixelInfo.

In the RemoveAlphaChannel case of SetImageAlphaChannel, ConformPixelInfo is relocated to execute before GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels, so any cache-side effects of pixel conformance complete before the row pointer q is acquired, ensuring q remains valid through its subsequent use in FlattenPixelInfo.

payload.summary: Use-after-free in SetImageAlphaChannel: ConformPixelInfo is called after GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels, and its internal cache operations can invalidate the acquired row pointer q before it is used in FlattenPixelInfo.
payload.mechanism: In the RemoveAlphaChannel path of SetImageAlphaChannel, GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels acquires a pointer q to a row in the image pixel cache. In the vulnerable ordering, ConformPixelInfo is subsequently called to conform the background pixel. Internally, ConformPixelInfo can trigger pixel cache reallocation or invalidation on the same image (e.g., through AcquireVirtualPixels or related cache-acquisition paths that may displace the existing cache view). This renders q a dangling pointer. The later call to FlattenPixelInfo then dereferences q, constituting a use-after-free (CWE-416). The fix reorders the two calls so that ConformPixelInfo executes before GetCacheViewAuthenticPixels, guaranteeing that any cache-modifying side effects are resolved before the row pointer is acquired.

payload.summary: Credentials updated in the URL via override_login were not URL-encoded, causing special characters in user or password to corrupt the URL structure during redirects.
payload.mechanism: In the override_login function in lib/url.c, when user or password credentials changed (e.g. during an HTTP redirect with -L following), curl_url_set was called with a flags argument of 0 for both CURLUPART_USER and CURLUPART_PASSWORD. This placed the raw credential strings directly into the URL object without percent-encoding. When credentials contained URL-significant characters such as '#' (fragment delimiter), the password portion after '#' was interpreted as a URL fragment, corrupting the URL. During a redirect the corrupted URL would produce an incorrect request, and the credentials could be partially exposed. The fix passes CURLU_URLENCODE as the flags argument so that credentials are properly percent-encoded before being stored in the URL.

observation_payloads.snippet:
600a8cded4 url: make the updated credentials URL-encoded in the URL
05e4a3026d Replaced 127.0.0.1 with %HOSTIP where possible
33bea767eb Convert (most of) the test data files into genuine XML.  A handful still are not, due mainly to the lack of support for XML character entities (e.g. & => & ).  This will make it easier to validate test files using tools like xmllint, as well as edit and view them using XML tools.
da58d03ff7 Venkat Akella found out that libcurl did not like HTTP responses that simply responded with a single status line and no headers nor body. Starting now, a HTTP response on a persistent connection (i.e not set to be closed after the response has been taken care of) must have Content-Length or chunked encoding set, or libcurl will simply assume that there is no body.
5d9fc28fa7 Modified the default HTTP headers used by libcurl:

payload.pattern: curl_url_set flags changed from 0 to CURLU_URLENCODE for user and password parts

payload.description: curl_url_set is called with flags=0 for CURLUPART_USER and CURLUPART_PASSWORD, placing raw unencoded credentials directly into the URL object.
curl_url_set is called with the CURLU_URLENCODE flag for both CURLUPART_USER and CURLUPART_PASSWORD, ensuring credentials are percent-encoded before insertion into the URL.

payload.summary: Credentials updated in the URL via override_login were not URL-encoded, causing special characters in user or password to corrupt the URL structure during redirects.
payload.mechanism: In the override_login function in lib/url.c, when user or password credentials changed (e.g. during an HTTP redirect with -L following), curl_url_set was called with a flags argument of 0 for both CURLUPART_USER and CURLUPART_PASSWORD. This placed the raw credential strings directly into the URL object without percent-encoding. When credentials contained URL-significant characters such as '#' (fragment delimiter), the password portion after '#' was interpreted as a URL fragment, corrupting the URL. During a redirect the corrupted URL would produce an incorrect request, and the credentials could be partially exposed. The fix passes CURLU_URLENCODE as the flags argument so that credentials are properly percent-encoded before being stored in the URL.

payload.summary: Combining -i (--include) with -J (--remote-header-name) allowed a server-controlled Content-Disposition filename to trigger rename() against an already-opened output file, enabling file overwrite.
payload.mechanism: When -i and -J are combined, HTTP response headers are written to the output stream. If the server sends a Content-Disposition header after the output file has already been opened (by prior Content-Disposition header or -O), tool_header_cb executes rename(outs->filename, filename) where filename originates from the attacker-controlled HTTP header. This allows a malicious server to overwrite arbitrary files on the local filesystem.

payload.pattern: rename removed; early bail-out on outs->stream already set
guard added: reject -i when --remote-header-name (-J) is active

payload.description: tool_header_cb executes rename(outs->filename, filename) when a Content-Disposition header is received and outs->stream is already set, where filename is parsed from the server-controlled HTTP header, without validating that -i and -J are not simultaneously active.

getparameter case 'i' now checks config->content_disposition and returns PARAM_BAD_USE when both --include (-i) and --remote-header-name (-J) are specified, preventing the dangerous combination at argument-parsing time.

tool_header_cb, when a Content-Disposition filename is parsed and outs->stream is already set, immediately frees filename and returns failure instead of attempting rename(), providing defense-in-depth against multiple Content-Disposition headers.

payload.summary: Combining -i (--include) with -J (--remote-header-name) allowed a server-controlled Content-Disposition filename to trigger rename() against an already-opened output file, enabling file overwrite.
payload.mechanism: When -i and -J are combined, HTTP response headers are written to the output stream. If the server sends a Content-Disposition header after the output file has already been opened (by prior Content-Disposition header or -O), tool_header_cb executes rename(outs->filename, filename) where filename originates from the attacker-controlled HTTP header. This allows a malicious server to overwrite arbitrary files on the local filesystem.

payload.summary: Use-after-free via dangling `data->state.lastconnect` pointer: the Curl_easy handle cached a raw `struct connectdata *` pointer that outlived the connection object, causing subsequent pointer dereference on freed/reused memory.
payload.mechanism: The `UrlState` struct in `lib/urldata.h` declared `struct connectdata *lastconnect` as a raw pointer field. In `multi_done` (lib/multi.c), the code stored a connection pointer (`data->state.lastconnect = conn`) after returning a connection to the cache, and set it to `NULL` when the return failed. However, connections are heap-allocated and can be freed after being evicted from the connection cache while the handle still holds the pointer value. When `Curl_getconnectinfo` later tested `data->state.lastconnect` for truthiness and dereferenced it directly, the pointer could reference freed or reallocated memory. The `conn_is_conn` callback also compared the cached pointer against live cache entries via pointer equality (`conn == f->tofind`), which could match a stale pointer or fail to match a reallocated connection at the same address. The commit message confirms this is a use-after-free (CVE-2020-8231, CWE-416). The fix replaces the raw pointer with `long lastconnect_id` — a connection identifier — and resolves the identifier back to a live `struct connectdata *` through the connection cache each time access is needed, ensuring the pointer is always valid at the point of use.

3c9e021f86 Curl_easy: remember last connection by id, not by pointer

payload.pattern: struct connectdata *lastconnect
conn == f->tofind
data->state.lastconnect
data->state.lastconnect = conn

payload.description: The UrlState struct field `lastconnect` is declared as `struct connectdata *lastconnect`, storing a raw pointer to a heap-allocated connection object that can be freed and returned to a connection cache while the pointer value is still retained in the Curl_easy handle's state.

The `conn_is_conn` callback in `lib/connect.c` compared the cached pointer `f->tofind` against connection pointers via direct pointer equality (`conn == f->tofind`), and `Curl_getconnectinfo` dereferenced `data->state.lastconnect` directly after a truthiness check, both using the potentially-dangling pointer without validating that the underlying connection object remained alive.

The UrlState struct field is changed from `struct connectdata *lastconnect` to `long lastconnect_id`, replacing a raw pointer with a stable numeric connection identifier. The sentinel value changes from `NULL` to `-1`. All comparison and assignment sites are updated to use the connection ID (`conn->connection_id`) instead of the pointer value.

The `connfind` struct fields change from `struct connectdata *tofind` / `bool found` to `long id_tofind` / `struct connectdata *found`. The `conn_is_conn` callback now compares `conn->connection_id == f->id_tofind` and stores the live pointer only when a match is found (`f->found = conn`). `Curl_getconnectinfo` resolves the connection pointer from the cache at access time (`c = find.found`), ensuring the pointer is valid because it is obtained from a live cache entry.

payload.summary: Use-after-free via dangling `data->state.lastconnect` pointer: the Curl_easy handle cached a raw `struct connectdata *` pointer that outlived the connection object, causing subsequent pointer dereference on freed/reused memory.
payload.mechanism: The `UrlState` struct in `lib/urldata.h` declared `struct connectdata *lastconnect` as a raw pointer field. In `multi_done` (lib/multi.c), the code stored a connection pointer (`data->state.lastconnect = conn`) after returning a connection to the cache, and set it to `NULL` when the return failed. However, connections are heap-allocated and can be freed after being evicted from the connection cache while the handle still holds the pointer value. When `Curl_getconnectinfo` later tested `data->state.lastconnect` for truthiness and dereferenced it directly, the pointer could reference freed or reallocated memory. The `conn_is_conn` callback also compared the cached pointer against live cache entries via pointer equality (`conn == f->tofind`), which could match a stale pointer or fail to match a reallocated connection at the same address. The commit message confirms this is a use-after-free (CVE-2020-8231, CWE-416). The fix replaces the raw pointer with `long lastconnect_id` — a connection identifier — and resolves the identifier back to a live `struct connectdata *` through the connection cache each time access is needed, ensuring the pointer is always valid at the point of use.

payload.summary: Silent truncation of uwsgi packet size due to apr_uint16_t type without bounds guard
payload.mechanism: In uwsgi_send_headers(), the variable pktsize was declared as apr_uint16_t while headerlen was an apr_size_t. When the accumulated HTTP header length (headerlen - 4) exceeded 65535, the assignment pktsize = headerlen - 4 silently truncated the value. This truncated value was then written into the 2-byte uwsgi packet header, producing a uwsgi protocol frame whose declared size did not match the actual serialized data size. No bounds check existed to reject oversized headers before the truncation occurred.

diff --git a/CHANGES b/CHANGES
index bbd6d1ec64..87fbed9786 100644
--- a/CHANGES
+++ b/CHANGES
@@ -1,83 +1,86 @@
                                                          -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 Changes with Apache 2.5.1
 
+  *) mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K (hard
+     protocol limit).  [Yann Ylavic]
+
   *) mod_dav: Some DAV extensions, like CalDAV, specify both document
      elements and property elements that need to be taken into account
      when generating a property. The document element and property element
      are made available in the dav_liveprop_elem structure under the
      DAV_PROP_ELEMENT key in the resource pool. [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mod_dav: Add utility functions dav_validate_root_ns(),
      dav_find_child_ns(), dav_find_next_ns(), dav_find_attr_ns() and
      dav_find_attr() so that other modules get to play too.
      [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mod_http2: 
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/200>: 
      "LimitRequestFields 0" now disables the limit, as documented.
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/201>: 
      Do not count repeated headers with same name against the field
      count limit. The are merged internally, as if sent in a single HTTP/1 line.
      [Stefan Eissing]
 
   *) mod_http2: Avoid segfaults in case of handling certain responses for
      already aborted connections.  [Stefan Eissing, Ruediger Pluem]
 
   *) core: Remove support for the Content-MD5 header, removed in RFC7231.
      Functions ap_md5digest() and ap_md5contextT

 Changes with Apache 2.4.44
 
+  *) mod_proxy_uwsgi: Error out on HTTP header larger than 16K (hard

   *) mod_http2: 
      Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/200>:

0c543e3f5b Merge r1880205, r1880214 from trunk:

payload.pattern: apr_uint16_t pktsize, keylen, vallen
if (pktsize > APR_UINT16_MAX)
pktsize = headerlen - 4
apr_uint16_t pktsize, keylen, vallen
if (pktsize > APR_UINT16_MAX)
pktsize = headerlen - 4

payload.description: pktsize is declared as apr_uint16_t (16-bit unsigned), causing silent truncation when headerlen - 4 exceeds APR_UINT16_MAX (65535), and no bounds check is performed before writing the truncated value into the 2-byte uwsgi packet header
pktsize type widened from apr_uint16_t to apr_size_t to prevent silent truncation of header sizes exceeding 65535
Explicit guard check if (pktsize > APR_UINT16_MAX) added early, before buffer allocation, returning HTTP_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR when the uwsgi 16K protocol limit is exceeded
if (pktsize > APR_UINT16_MAX) — rejects oversized HTTP headers before serialization into the uwsgi protocol frame, a guard absent in the vulnerable code

payload.summary: Silent truncation of uwsgi packet size due to apr_uint16_t type without bounds guard
payload.mechanism: In uwsgi_send_headers(), the variable pktsize was declared as apr_uint16_t while headerlen was an apr_size_t. When the accumulated HTTP header length (headerlen - 4) exceeded 65535, the assignment pktsize = headerlen - 4 silently truncated the value. This truncated value was then written into the 2-byte uwsgi packet header, producing a uwsgi protocol frame whose declared size did not match the actual serialized data size. No bounds check existed to reject oversized headers before the truncation occurred.

payload.summary: mod_remoteip used c->client_addr as the root trusted address for X-Forwarded-For verification; during internal redirects the same connection-level address is reused, causing the XFF chain to be re-evaluated from the trusted proxy address rather than resuming from the already-processed useragent_ip.
payload.mechanism: In remoteip_modify_request, the temporary socket address temp_sa was initialized to c->client_addr (the raw TCP client address of the connection). During an internal redirect triggered by ErrorDocument, the handler executes a second time on the same request_rec. Since c->client_addr is connection-scoped and unchanged across subrequests, the X-Forwarded-For verification loop restarts from the trusted proxy's address. If c->client_addr matches a trusted proxy, the already-modified XFF header is iterated again from the beginning, causing the useragent_ip to be incorrectly overwritten with an earlier trusted IP instead of preserving the correct client IP derived from the original request.

obs-git-show-stat-d0c4af10ab71.snippet:
commit d0c4af10ab713734de906b5634cfc15cd370fdf4
Author: Jim Jagielski <jim@apache.org>
Date:   Tue Nov 1 11:55:34 2016 +0000

    Merge r1688399 from trunk:
    
    mod_remoteip: Use r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address for verifying.
    
    This fixes issue resulting in setting of bad useragent_ip when internal
    redirection has been generated as response to the request (typically as
    result of "ErrorDocument 40x").
    
    In this case, the original request has been handled by mod_remoteip and its
    useragent_ip has been changed properly, but when internal redirection
    to ErrorDocument has been generated later, the mod_remoteip's handler has been
    executed again with *the same* c->client_addr as in the original request. If
    c->client_addr IP is trusted, this results in bad useragent_ip being set.
    
    When using r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address instead of
    c->client_addr, the internal redirection uses the first non-trusted
    IP in this particular case, so it won't change the r->useragent_ip during
    the internal redirection to ErrorDocument.
    
    Submitted by: jkaluza
    Reviewed/backported by: jim
    
    
    git-svn-id: https://svn.apache.org/repos/asf/httpd/httpd/branches/2.4.x@1767483 13f79535-47bb-0310-9956-ffa450edef68

 CHANGES                         | 6 +++++-
 STATUS                          | 6 ------
 modules/metadata/mod_remoteip.c | 2 +-
 3 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

observation_payloads.claim: git show --stat for fix commit d0c4af10ab713734de906b5634cfc15cd370fdf4
observation_payloads.snippet:
commit d0c4af10ab713734de906b5634cfc15cd370fdf4
Author: Jim Jagielski <jim@apache.org>
Date:   Tue Nov 1 11:55:34 2016 +0000

    Merge r1688399 from trunk:
    
    mod_remoteip: Use r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address for verifying.
    
    This fixes issue resulting in setting of bad useragent_ip when internal
    redirection has been generated as response to the request (typically as
    result of "ErrorDocument 40x").
    
    In this case, the original request has been handled by mod_remoteip and its
    useragent_ip has been changed properly, but when internal redirection
    to ErrorDocument has been generated later, the mod_remoteip's handler has been
    executed again with *the same* c->client_addr as in the original request. If
    c->client_addr IP is trusted, this results in bad useragent_ip being set.
    
    When using r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address instead of
    c->client_addr, the internal redirection uses the first non-trusted
    IP in this particular case, so it won't change the r->useragent_ip during
    the internal redirection to ErrorDocument.
    
    Submitted by: jkaluza
    Reviewed/backported by: jim
    
    
    git-svn-id: https://svn.apache.org/repos/asf/httpd/httpd/branches/2.4.x@1767483 13f79535-47bb-0310-9956-ffa450edef68

 CHANGES                         | 6 +++++-
 STATUS                          | 6 ------
 modules/metadata/mod_remoteip.c | 2 +-
 3 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

payload.pattern: temp_sa = r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr;

payload.description: temp_sa is unconditionally initialized to c->client_addr, the connection's TCP client address, which remains unchanged across internal redirects. When c->client_addr is a trusted proxy IP, the X-Forwarded-For verification loop restarts from this trusted address during subrequests, leading to incorrect useragent_ip derivation.

temp_sa is initialized to r->useragent_addr when it is non-NULL, falling back to c->client_addr only when no useragent_addr has been set. This preserves the already-processed client identity across internal redirects, preventing the XFF chain from being re-evaluated from the trusted proxy.

The ternary expression r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr guards against NULL r->useragent_addr by falling back to c->client_addr for the initial request where no useragent_addr has yet been established.

payload.summary: mod_remoteip used c->client_addr as the root trusted address for X-Forwarded-For verification; during internal redirects the same connection-level address is reused, causing the XFF chain to be re-evaluated from the trusted proxy address rather than resuming from the already-processed useragent_ip.
payload.mechanism: In remoteip_modify_request, the temporary socket address temp_sa was initialized to c->client_addr (the raw TCP client address of the connection). During an internal redirect triggered by ErrorDocument, the handler executes a second time on the same request_rec. Since c->client_addr is connection-scoped and unchanged across subrequests, the X-Forwarded-For verification loop restarts from the trusted proxy's address. If c->client_addr matches a trusted proxy, the already-modified XFF header is iterated again from the beginning, causing the useragent_ip to be incorrectly overwritten with an earlier trusted IP instead of preserving the correct client IP derived from the original request.

payload.summary: Unconditional header counting in h2_stream_add_header against LimitRequestFields allows HTTP request smuggling via inconsistent interpretation (CWE-444)
payload.mechanism: In h2_stream_add_header() in modules/http2/h2_stream.c, the counter stream->request_headers_added was incremented unconditionally (++stream->request_headers_added) for every received HTTP/2 header before any merging or deduplication logic. Repeated headers with the same name (e.g. Cookie) are merged internally by h2_req_add_header() but were still counted against the LimitRequestFields limit. This caused an inconsistency between the HTTP/2 layer's field count and the actual number of distinct fields the server processes, enabling HTTP request smuggling (CWE-444). Additionally, LimitRequestFields set to 0 (which should disable the limit) was not properly honored because the counter was incremented before the limit check.

* hooks trigger special behaviour when keepalives is 0.

/* We signal that this connection will be closed after the request.

                                                          -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 Changes with Apache 2.4.44
 
+  *) mod_http2: The module now handles master/secondary connections and has marked
+     methods according to use. [Stefan Eissing]
+
   *) core: Drop an invalid Last-Modified header value coming
      from a FCGI/CGI script instead of replacing it with Unix epoch.
      [Luca Toscano]
 
   *) Add support for strict content-length parsing through addition of
      ap_parse_strict_length() [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_proxy_fcgi: ProxyFCGISetEnvIf unsets variables when expression
      evaluates to false.  PR64365. [Michael König <mail ikoenig.net>]

      leaking (or long lived) temporary file. PR 64452. [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_ssl: Fix a race condition and possible c

--- a/CHANGES
+++ b/CHANGES
@@ -1,84 +1,92 @@
                                                          -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 Changes with Apache 2.5.1
 
 
+  *) mod_http2: 
+     Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/200>: 
+     "LimitRequestFields 0" now disables the limit, as documented.
+     Fixes <https://github.com/icing/mod_h2/issues/201>: 
+     Do not count repeated headers with same name against the field
+     count limit. The are merged internally, as if sent in a single HTTP/1 line.
+     [Stefan Eissing]
+
   *) mod_http2: Avoid segfaults in case of handling certain responses for
      already aborted connections.  [Stefan Eissing, Ruediger Pluem]
 
   *) core: Remove support for the Content-MD5 header, removed in RFC7231.
      Functions ap_md5digest() and ap_md5contextTo64() removed, and
      ContentDigest directive. [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mod_dav: Allow other DAV modules to use dav_get_resource().
      [Graham Leggett]
 
   *) mpm_common: remove ap_mpm_unregister_poll_callback() and
      mpm_unregister_poll_callback hook. [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_proxy_http: add asynchronous handling of Upgrade(d) protocols,
      where idle connections are returned to the MPM and rescheduled on
      another thread when ready. [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_dav: Add metho

b5ef6235a3   *) mod_proxy_http2: the "ping" proxy parameter      (see <https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.4/mod/mod_proxy.html>) is now used      when checking the liveliness of a new or reused h2 connection to the backend.      With short durations, this makes load-balancing more responsive. The module      will hold back requests until ping conditions are met, using features of the      HTTP/2 protocol alone.  Changes with Apache 2.4.44
 
+  *) mod_http2: The module now handles master/secondary connections and has marked
+     methods according to use. [Stefan Eissing]
+
   *) core: Drop an invalid Last-Modified header value coming
      from a FCGI/CGI script instead of replacing it with Unix epoch.
      [Luca Toscano]
 
   *) Add support for strict content-length parsing through addition of
      ap_parse_strict_length() [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_proxy_fcgi: ProxyFCGISetEnvIf unsets variables when expression
      evaluates to false.  PR64365. [Michael König <mail ikoenig.net>]
 
   *) mod_proxy_http: flush spooled request body in one go to avoid
      leaking (or long lived) temporary file. PR 64452. [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_ssl: Fix a race condition and possible c

 Changes with Apache 2.5.1

      ContentDigest directive. [Graham Leggett]

   *) mod_proxy_http: add asynchronous handling of Upgrade(d) protocols,
      where idle connections are returned to the MPM and rescheduled on
      another thread when ready. [Yann Ylavic]
 
   *) mod_dav: Add metho

payload.pattern: ++stream->request_headers_added removed; int error = 0, was_added = 0 added
r->protocol = "HTTP/2.0"; r->proto_num = HTTP_VERSION(2, 0) added

payload.description: h2_stream_add_header() unconditionally increments stream->request_headers_added for every header received, before deduplication or merging, causing repeated headers (e.g. Cookie) to be counted multiple times against the field limit

h2_stream_add_header() now tracks whether a header was genuinely new via pwas_added/was_added, incrementing stream->request_headers_added only when a header is actually added (not merged as a duplicate), and honors limit_req_fields > 0 guard before checking the limit

payload.summary: Unconditional header counting in h2_stream_add_header against LimitRequestFields allows HTTP request smuggling via inconsistent interpretation (CWE-444)
payload.mechanism: In h2_stream_add_header() in modules/http2/h2_stream.c, the counter stream->request_headers_added was incremented unconditionally (++stream->request_headers_added) for every received HTTP/2 header before any merging or deduplication logic. Repeated headers with the same name (e.g. Cookie) are merged internally by h2_req_add_header() but were still counted against the LimitRequestFields limit. This caused an inconsistency between the HTTP/2 layer's field count and the actual number of distinct fields the server processes, enabling HTTP request smuggling (CWE-444). Additionally, LimitRequestFields set to 0 (which should disable the limit) was not properly honored because the counter was incremented before the limit check.

payload.summary: KVM SEV lacked cache flush on mmu_notifier invalidation; __kvm_handle_hva_range had no on_unlock callback mechanism to flush encrypted cachelines when guest memory was reclaimed.
payload.mechanism: In virt/kvm/kvm_main.c, __kvm_handle_hva_range would unlock the MMU lock after HVA range invalidation but had no hook to invoke an architecture-specific memory-reclaim callback. The kvm_hva_range struct lacked an on_unlock field, and the mmu_notifier invalidation path (kvm_mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start) had no way to trigger a cache flush. For SEV guests, this meant wbinvd_on_all_cpus() was never called when encrypted memory pages were unmapped and reclaimed, violating AMD SEV cache coherency requirements (APM vol.2 §15.34.7).

obs-git-show-stat-683412ccf612.snippet:
commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
Author: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 21 03:14:07 2022 +0000

    KVM: SEV: add cache flush to solve SEV cache incoherency issues
    
    Flush the CPU caches when memory is reclaimed from an SEV guest (where
    reclaim also includes it being unmapped from KVM's memslots).  Due to lack
    of coherency for SEV encrypted memory, failure to flush results in silent
    data corruption if userspace is malicious/broken and doesn't ensure SEV
    guest memory is properly pinned and unpinned.
    
    Cache coherency is not enforced across the VM boundary in SEV (AMD APM
    vol.2 Section 15.34.7). Confidential cachelines, generated by confidential
    VM guests have to be explicitly flushed on the host side. If a memory page
    containing dirty confidential cachelines was released by VM and reallocated
    to another user, the cachelines may corrupt the new user at a later time.
    
    KVM takes a shortcut by assuming all confidential memory remain pinned
    until the end of VM lifetime. Therefore, KVM does not flush cache at
    mmu_notifier invalidation events. Because of this incorrect assumption and
    the lack of cache flushing, malicous userspace can crash the host kernel:
    creating a malicious VM and continuously allocates/releases unpinned
    confidential memory pages when the VM is running.
    
    Add cache flush operations to mmu_notifier operations to ensure that any
    physical memory leaving the guest VM get flushed. In particular, hook
    mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start and mmu_notifier_release events and
    flush cache accordingly. The hook after releasing the mmu lock to avoid
    contention with other vCPUs.
    
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
    Suggested-by: Sean Christpherson <seanjc@google.com>
    Reported-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Message-Id: <20220421031407.2516575-4-mizhang@google.com>

observation_payloads.claim: git show --stat for fix commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
observation_payloads.snippet:
commit 683412ccf61294d727ead4a73d97397396e69a6b
Author: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 21 03:14:07 2022 +0000

    KVM: SEV: add cache flush to solve SEV cache incoherency issues
    
    Flush the CPU caches when memory is reclaimed from an SEV guest (where
    reclaim also includes it being unmapped from KVM's memslots).  Due to lack
    of coherency for SEV encrypted memory, failure to flush results in silent
    data corruption if userspace is malicious/broken and doesn't ensure SEV
    guest memory is properly pinned and unpinned.
    
    Cache coherency is not enforced across the VM boundary in SEV (AMD APM
    vol.2 Section 15.34.7). Confidential cachelines, generated by confidential
    VM guests have to be explicitly flushed on the host side. If a memory page
    containing dirty confidential cachelines was released by VM and reallocated
    to another user, the cachelines may corrupt the new user at a later time.
    
    KVM takes a shortcut by assuming all confidential memory remain pinned
    until the end of VM lifetime. Therefore, KVM does not flush cache at
    mmu_notifier invalidation events. Because of this incorrect assumption and
    the lack of cache flushing, malicous userspace can crash the host kernel:
    creating a malicious VM and continuously allocates/releases unpinned
    confidential memory pages when the VM is running.
    
    Add cache flush operations to mmu_notifier operations to ensure that any
    physical memory leaving the guest VM get flushed. In particular, hook
    mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start and mmu_notifier_release events and
    flush cache accordingly. The hook after releasing the mmu lock to avoid
    contention with other vCPUs.
    
    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
    Suggested-by: Sean Christpherson <seanjc@google.com>
    Reported-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mingwei Zhang <mizhang@google.com>
    Message-Id: <20220421031407.2516575-4-mizhang@google.com>

payload.pattern: on_unlock callback invoked after MMU unlock
on_unlock_fn_t typedef and on_unlock field added to kvm_hva_range
.on_unlock = kvm_arch_guest_memory_reclaimed set in mmu_notifier range start

payload.description: __kvm_handle_hva_range in virt/kvm/kvm_main.c unlocked the MMU lock after HVA range invalidation without calling any cache-flush callback. The kvm_hva_range struct had no on_unlock field, so no architecture-specific memory-reclaim handler could be invoked during mmu_notifier invalidation. This left SEV confidential cachelines in the CPU cache after guest memory was unmapped and reclaimed.

The kvm_hva_range struct gains an on_unlock callback field (on_unlock_fn_t). After __kvm_handle_hva_range unlocks the MMU lock, it now checks if range->on_unlock is set and not a NULL function, and if so calls range->on_unlock(kvm) to invoke an architecture-specific memory-reclaim handler.

kvm_mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start sets .on_unlock = kvm_arch_guest_memory_reclaimed on the kvm_hva_range, ensuring that when __kvm_handle_hva_range finishes HVA invalidation and unlocks the MMU, kvm_arch_guest_memory_reclaimed is called. For SEV guests this dispatches to sev_guest_memory_reclaimed which calls wbinvd_on_all_cpus() to flush encrypted cachelines.

payload.summary: KVM SEV lacked cache flush on mmu_notifier invalidation; __kvm_handle_hva_range had no on_unlock callback mechanism to flush encrypted cachelines when guest memory was reclaimed.
payload.mechanism: In virt/kvm/kvm_main.c, __kvm_handle_hva_range would unlock the MMU lock after HVA range invalidation but had no hook to invoke an architecture-specific memory-reclaim callback. The kvm_hva_range struct lacked an on_unlock field, and the mmu_notifier invalidation path (kvm_mmu_notifier_invalidate_range_start) had no way to trigger a cache flush. For SEV guests, this meant wbinvd_on_all_cpus() was never called when encrypted memory pages were unmapped and reclaimed, violating AMD SEV cache coherency requirements (APM vol.2 §15.34.7).

payload.summary: Unsigned integer wraparound in legacy_parse_param() bypasses cumulative-option-size boundary check
payload.mechanism: In legacy_parse_param() at fs/fs_context.c, the expression 'PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' is evaluated with unsigned integer arithmetic (size is size_t). When 'size' grows large enough that 'PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' should be negative, the unsigned subtraction wraps around to a value near SIZE_MAX. The guard 'len > PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' therefore compares 'len' against a huge unsigned value, always evaluating to false and never rejecting oversized cumulative options. The fix rewrites the check as 'size + len + 2 > PAGE_SIZE', using addition to avoid unsigned wraparound entirely.

payload.pattern: if (len > PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size)

payload.description: The boundary check 'len > PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' computes 'PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' with unsigned arithmetic. When 'size' (a size_t) is large enough that the subtraction would yield a negative value, the unsigned wraparound produces a value near SIZE_MAX, making the guard always evaluate to false for any practical 'len', thereby allowing cumulative option data to be written past the PAGE_SIZE limit.

The boundary check is replaced with 'size + len + 2 > PAGE_SIZE', which uses addition instead of subtraction, eliminating the unsigned wraparound by comparing the total required space directly against PAGE_SIZE.

payload.summary: Unsigned integer wraparound in legacy_parse_param() bypasses cumulative-option-size boundary check
payload.mechanism: In legacy_parse_param() at fs/fs_context.c, the expression 'PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' is evaluated with unsigned integer arithmetic (size is size_t). When 'size' grows large enough that 'PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' should be negative, the unsigned subtraction wraps around to a value near SIZE_MAX. The guard 'len > PAGE_SIZE - 2 - size' therefore compares 'len' against a huge unsigned value, always evaluating to false and never rejecting oversized cumulative options. The fix rewrites the check as 'size + len + 2 > PAGE_SIZE', using addition to avoid unsigned wraparound entirely.

payload.summary: check_atomic() passes a non-negative load_reg to check_mem_access(BPF_READ), bypassing the pointer-value safety gate in check_stack_read_fixed_off() that only rejects when dst_regno < 0, allowing unprivileged BPF programs to leak spilled kernel pointer values during atomic fetch operations.
payload.mechanism: In check_atomic(), for BPF_FETCH atomics the variable load_reg is set to a non-negative destination register number. The vulnerable code calls check_mem_access(env, insn_idx, insn->dst_reg, insn->off, BPF_SIZE(insn->code), BPF_READ, load_reg, true) with load_reg >= 0. Inside check_mem_access, this eventually reaches check_stack_read_fixed_off(), which contains a gate: if dst_regno < 0 && __is_pointer_value(env, reg_type) then the program is rejected. With load_reg >= 0, dst_regno is non-negative, so the pointer-value rejection gate is bypassed. This allows read access to a stack slot containing a spilled kernel pointer, and the register type propagation converts the pointer into a scalar, which can then be exported out of the BPF program into a map value — leaking the kernel address.

obs-git-show-stat-7d3baf0afa3a.snippet:
commit 7d3baf0afa3aa9102d6a521a8e4c41888bb79882
Author: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Date:   Tue Dec 7 12:51:56 2021 +0000

    bpf: Fix kernel address leakage in atomic fetch
    
    The change in commit 37086bfdc737 ("bpf: Propagate stack bounds to registers
    in atomics w/ BPF_FETCH") around check_mem_access() handling is buggy since
    this would allow for unprivileged users to leak kernel pointers. For example,
    an atomic fetch/and with -1 on a stack destination which holds a spilled
    pointer will migrate the spilled register type into a scalar, which can then
    be exported out of the program (since scalar != pointer) by dumping it into
    a map value.
    
    The original implementation of XADD was preventing this situation by using
    a double call to check_mem_access() one with BPF_READ and a subsequent one
    with BPF_WRITE, in both cases passing -1 as a placeholder value instead of
    register as per XADD semantics since it didn't contain a value fetch. The
    BPF_READ also included a check in check_stack_read_fixed_off() which rejects
    the program if the stack slot is of __is_pointer_value() if dst_regno < 0.
    The latter is to distinguish whether we're dealing with a regular stack spill/
    fill or some arithmetical operation which is disallowed on non-scalars, see
    also 6e7e63cbb023 ("bpf: Forbid XADD on spilled pointers for unprivileged
    users") for more context on check_mem_access() and its handling of placeholder
    value -1.
    
    One minimally intrusive option to fix the leak is for the BPF_FETCH case to
    initially check the BPF_READ case via check_mem_access() with -1 as register,
    followed by the actual load case with non-negative load_reg to propagate
    stack bounds to registers.
    
    Fixes: 37086bfdc737 ("bpf: Propagate stack bounds to registers in atomics w/ BPF_FETCH")
    Reported-by: <n4ke4mry@gmail.com>
    Acked-by: Brendan Jackman <jackmanb@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Daniel Bork

obs-git-show-unified-7d3baf0afa3a.snippet:
commit 7d3baf0afa3aa9102d6a521a8e4c41888bb79882
Author: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Date:   Tue Dec 7 12:51:56 2021 +0000

    bpf: Fix kernel address leakage in atomic fetch
    
    The change in commit 37086bfdc737 ("bpf: Propagate stack bounds to registers
    in atomics w/ BPF_FETCH") around check_mem_access() handling is buggy since
    this would allow for unprivileged users to leak kernel pointers. For example,
    an atomic fetch/and with -1 on a stack destination which holds a spilled
    pointer will migrate the spilled register type into a scalar, which can then
    be exported out of the program (since scalar != pointer) by dumping it into
    a map value.
    
    The original implementation of XADD was preventing this situation by using
    a double call to check_mem_access() one with BPF_READ and a subsequent one
    with BPF_WRITE, in both cases passing -1 as a placeholder value instead of
    register as per XADD semantics since it didn't contain a value fetch. The
    BPF_READ also included a check in check_stack_read_fixed_off() which rejects
    the program if the stack slot is of __is_pointer_value() if dst_regno < 0.
    The latter is to distinguish whether we're dealing with a regular stack spill/
    fill or some arithmetical operation which is disallowed on non-scalars, see
    also 6e7e63cbb023 ("bpf: Forbid XADD on spilled pointers for unprivileged
    users") for more context on check_mem_access() and its handling of placeholder
    value -1.
    
    One minimally intrusive option to fix the leak is for the BPF_FETCH case to
    initially check the BPF_READ case via check_mem_access() with -1 as register,
    followed by the actual load case with non-negative load_reg to propagate
    stack bounds to registers.
    
    Fixes: 37086bfdc737 ("bpf: Propagate stack bounds to registers in atomics w/ BPF_FETCH")
    Reported-by: <n4ke4mry@gmail.com>
    Acked-by: Brendan Jackman <jackmanb@google.com>
    Signed-off-by: Daniel Bork

observation_payloads.snippet:
7d3baf0afa3a bpf: Fix kernel address leakage in atomic fetch
345e004d0233 bpf: Fix incorrect state pruning for <8B spill/fill
2fa7d94afc1a bpf: Fix the off-by-two error in range markings

payload.pattern: check_mem_access(env, insn_idx, insn->dst_reg, insn->off, BPF_SIZE(insn->code), BPF_READ, load_reg, true)

payload.description: check_mem_access() is called with BPF_READ and load_reg (non-negative for BPF_FETCH) directly, without an additional call using -1 as register to trigger the pointer-value safety check in check_stack_read_fixed_off(). The removed line passes load_reg instead of -1 as the register argument to check_mem_access.

A first check_mem_access(..., BPF_READ, -1, true) call is introduced with -1 as the register placeholder, triggering the pointer-value rejection gate in check_stack_read_fixed_off() before any register propagation occurs.

A second check_mem_access(..., BPF_READ, load_reg, true) call is added, gated by if (!err && load_reg >= 0), so register propagation from the fetch only occurs after the pointer-value safety check passes.

The condition load_reg >= 0 gates the second check_mem_access call in the fix, ensuring register propagation only occurs when a fetch destination register exists and only after the first check_mem_access(BPF_READ, -1) has passed the pointer-value safety check.

In the vulnerable code, when load_reg >= 0 (BPF_FETCH case), the dst_regno < 0 gate in check_stack_read_fixed_off() is bypassed, so __is_pointer_value() check that would reject reading spilled pointers is never executed for the BPF_READ access.

payload.summary: check_atomic() passes a non-negative load_reg to check_mem_access(BPF_READ), bypassing the pointer-value safety gate in check_stack_read_fixed_off() that only rejects when dst_regno < 0, allowing unprivileged BPF programs to leak spilled kernel pointer values during atomic fetch operations.
payload.mechanism: In check_atomic(), for BPF_FETCH atomics the variable load_reg is set to a non-negative destination register number. The vulnerable code calls check_mem_access(env, insn_idx, insn->dst_reg, insn->off, BPF_SIZE(insn->code), BPF_READ, load_reg, true) with load_reg >= 0. Inside check_mem_access, this eventually reaches check_stack_read_fixed_off(), which contains a gate: if dst_regno < 0 && __is_pointer_value(env, reg_type) then the program is rejected. With load_reg >= 0, dst_regno is non-negative, so the pointer-value rejection gate is bypassed. This allows read access to a stack slot containing a spilled kernel pointer, and the register type propagation converts the pointer into a scalar, which can then be exported out of the BPF program into a map value — leaking the kernel address.

payload.summary: Null pointer dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa() when bond->curr_active_slave is NULL
payload.mechanism: In bond_ipsec_add_sa(), rcu_dereference(bond->curr_active_slave) assigns the active slave pointer to 'slave'. When the bonding device has no real (active) slave, this returns NULL. The code then unconditionally dereferences 'slave->dev' on the next line, causing a null pointer dereference.

commit 105cd17a866017b45f3c45901b394c711c97bf40
Author:     Taehee Yoo <ap420073@gmail.com>
AuthorDate: Mon Jul 5 15:38:07 2021 +0000
Commit:     David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
CommitDate: Tue Jul 6 10:36:59 2021 -0700

    bonding: fix null dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa()
    
    If bond doesn't have real device, bond->curr_active_slave is null.
    But bond_ipsec_add_sa() dereferences bond->curr_active_slave without
    null checking.
    So, null-ptr-deref would occur.
    
    Tes

105cd17a8660 bonding: fix null dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa()

payload.pattern: slave = rcu_dereference(bond->curr_active_slave) followed by direct dereference slave->dev without null check; fix inserts if (!slave) guard

payload.description: slave pointer dereferenced via slave->dev without null check after rcu_dereference(bond->curr_active_slave) which can return NULL when no active slave exists
Add null check if (!slave) with rcu_read_unlock() and return -ENODEV before dereferencing slave->dev
The added if (!slave) guard checks whether the slave pointer is NULL before dereferencing it, preventing the null pointer dereference

payload.summary: Null pointer dereference in bond_ipsec_add_sa() when bond->curr_active_slave is NULL
payload.mechanism: In bond_ipsec_add_sa(), rcu_dereference(bond->curr_active_slave) assigns the active slave pointer to 'slave'. When the bonding device has no real (active) slave, this returns NULL. The code then unconditionally dereferences 'slave->dev' on the next line, causing a null pointer dereference.

payload.summary: Buffer under-allocation in sctp_make_strreset_req() due to summing unpadded sub-chunk lengths before chunk allocation
payload.mechanism: sctp_make_strreset_req() computes outlen and inlen as the lengths of the outgoing and incoming stream reset request payloads. It passes their raw sum (outlen + inlen) to sctp_make_reconf(), which calls _sctp_make_chunk() to allocate the chunk buffer using SCTP_PAD4(outlen + inlen). However, sctp_make_strreset_req() then independently appends each payload via sctp_addto_chunk(), which applies SCTP_PAD4 padding per call. When outlen and inlen have different 4-byte alignment offsets, SCTP_PAD4(outlen + inlen) can be strictly less than SCTP_PAD4(outlen) + SCTP_PAD4(inlen), causing the chunk buffer to be smaller than the total space actually consumed by the appended payloads, leading to a buffer overrun and BUG_ON.

a2d859e3fc97 sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk
a34dcbfa1475 sctp: prevent info leak in sctp_make_heartbeat()

payload.pattern: sctp_make_reconf(asoc, outlen + inlen)

payload.description: sctp_make_strreset_req() calls sctp_make_reconf() with the raw sum outlen + inlen without applying 4-byte alignment padding (SCTP_PAD4) to each component individually. Since sctp_addto_chunk() applies SCTP_PAD4 padding independently for each sub-chunk it appends, the total actual consumed length can exceed the allocated length when the individual components have different alignment offsets.

The fix applies SCTP_PAD4 to outlen and inlen individually before summing them: SCTP_PAD4(outlen) + SCTP_PAD4(inlen). This correctly accounts for the padding that sctp_addto_chunk() independently applies to each sub-chunk, ensuring the allocated chunk buffer is always large enough.

payload.summary: Buffer under-allocation in sctp_make_strreset_req() due to summing unpadded sub-chunk lengths before chunk allocation
payload.mechanism: sctp_make_strreset_req() computes outlen and inlen as the lengths of the outgoing and incoming stream reset request payloads. It passes their raw sum (outlen + inlen) to sctp_make_reconf(), which calls _sctp_make_chunk() to allocate the chunk buffer using SCTP_PAD4(outlen + inlen). However, sctp_make_strreset_req() then independently appends each payload via sctp_addto_chunk(), which applies SCTP_PAD4 padding per call. When outlen and inlen have different 4-byte alignment offsets, SCTP_PAD4(outlen + inlen) can be strictly less than SCTP_PAD4(outlen) + SCTP_PAD4(inlen), causing the chunk buffer to be smaller than the total space actually consumed by the appended payloads, leading to a buffer overrun and BUG_ON.

payload.summary: The bloom filter map operations struct (bloom_filter_map_ops) was initialized without a map_get_next_key callback, leaving the function pointer NULL. When the kernel's generic BPF map iteration code called map_get_next_key() on a bloom filter map, it dereferenced this NULL pointer, causing a kernel crash.
payload.mechanism: The struct bpf_map_ops instance bloom_filter_map_ops omitted the .map_get_next_key field assignment. In C, designated initializers for unspecified struct members are zero-initialized, producing a NULL function pointer. The kernel's generic bpf_map_get_next_key() syscall path (kernel/bpf/syscall.c) unconditionally invokes map->ops->map_get_next_key() without checking for NULL. When userspace called BPF_MAP_GET_NEXT_KEY on a bloom filter map, the kernel executed a NULL function pointer, resulting in a crash due to NULL pointer dereference.

payload.pattern: static int bloom_map_get_next_key(struct bpf_map *map, void *key, void *next_key) { return -EOPNOTSUPP; }
.map_get_next_key = bloom_map_get_next_key,

payload.description: The bloom_filter_map_ops struct was declared without a .map_get_next_key field assignment, causing the function pointer slot to be zero-initialized to NULL.
No bloom_map_get_next_key function existed for the bloom filter map type, so calling BPF_MAP_GET_NEXT_KEY on a bloom filter map would invoke a NULL function pointer.
A new bloom_map_get_next_key function is defined that returns -EOPNOTSUPP, providing a safe stub that prevents NULL pointer dereference.
The bloom_filter_map_ops struct now wires .map_get_next_key = bloom_map_get_next_key, registering the new callback so the kernel no longer dereferences a NULL function pointer.
The bloom_map_get_next_key stub returns -EOPNOTSUPP unconditionally, acting as a safe sentinel that prevents the kernel from performing unsupported iteration on bloom filter maps.
Before the fix, the bloom_filter_map_ops struct lacked any .map_get_next_key assignment, so the kernel's generic map_get_next_key path had no callback to call, resulting in a NULL dereference.

payload.summary: The bloom filter map operations struct (bloom_filter_map_ops) was initialized without a map_get_next_key callback, leaving the function pointer NULL. When the kernel's generic BPF map iteration code called map_get_next_key() on a bloom filter map, it dereferenced this NULL pointer, causing a kernel crash.
payload.mechanism: The struct bpf_map_ops instance bloom_filter_map_ops omitted the .map_get_next_key field assignment. In C, designated initializers for unspecified struct members are zero-initialized, producing a NULL function pointer. The kernel's generic bpf_map_get_next_key() syscall path (kernel/bpf/syscall.c) unconditionally invokes map->ops->map_get_next_key() without checking for NULL. When userspace called BPF_MAP_GET_NEXT_KEY on a bloom filter map, the kernel executed a NULL function pointer, resulting in a crash due to NULL pointer dereference.

payload.summary: Resource cleanup pointers (image, l_stream, l_codec, cstr_index) are initialized to NULL at function scope instead of being re-initialized per loop iteration, causing a double-free when a subsequent iteration fails on an invalid image after a prior successful iteration left the pointers non-NULL.
payload.mechanism: In the main function's image processing loop, four resource pointers (image, l_stream, l_codec, cstr_index) were declared and NULL-initialized at function scope. When processing a directory with a mix of valid and invalid images, a successful decode iteration sets these pointers to allocated values. If a subsequent iteration fails before reassigning these pointers, the loop's cleanup/error path calls destroy functions (opj_destroy_codec, opj_stream_destroy, opj_image_destroy, opj_destroy_cstr_index) on the still-non-NULL pointers from the previous iteration, resulting in a double-free. The fix moves the declarations into the loop body so each iteration starts with fresh NULL pointers.

payload.description: Variables image, l_stream, l_codec, and cstr_index are declared and NULL-initialized at function scope (lines 1319-1322), outside the image-processing for-loop. After a successful decode in one loop iteration populates these pointers with allocated resources, a subsequent iteration that fails on an invalid image may reach the cleanup code path with these pointers still holding the previous iteration's values, triggering a double-free when opj_destroy_codec / opj_stream_destroy / opj_image_destroy / opj_destroy_cstr_index are called on already-freed allocations.

The four variable declarations (image, l_stream, l_codec, cstr_index) are moved from function scope into the body of the for-loop (lines 1392-1395) so that they are re-initialized to NULL at the start of each iteration, preventing stale pointer values from a previous iteration from being used in cleanup/destroy calls when the current iteration fails.

payload.summary: Resource cleanup pointers (image, l_stream, l_codec, cstr_index) are initialized to NULL at function scope instead of being re-initialized per loop iteration, causing a double-free when a subsequent iteration fails on an invalid image after a prior successful iteration left the pointers non-NULL.

If a subsequent iteration fails before reassigning these pointers, the loop's cleanup/error path calls destroy functions (opj_destroy_codec, opj_stream_destroy, opj_image_destroy, opj_destroy_cstr_index) on the still-non-NULL pointers from the previous iteration, resulting in a double-free. The fix moves the declarations into the loop body so each iteration starts with fresh NULL pointers.

payload.summary: Insufficient heap buffer size estimate in JPEG 2000 encoder code block allocation, causing write beyond allocated buffer for certain encoding parameter combinations
payload.mechanism: The JPEG 2000 encoder allocates a heap buffer for compressed code block data using an estimate: a base padding constant plus the uncompressed block dimensions multiplied by sizeof(OPJ_UINT32). The base padding constant was empirically insufficient for certain encoding parameter combinations, causing the compressed byte stream to exceed the allocated heap buffer. The fix enlarges the buffer by increasing the base padding constant.

payload.summary: In pngtoimage(), image->x1 and image->y1 doubled image->x0/image->y0 in their computation when the -d option set a non-zero image offset, producing inflated endpoint values that caused a heap buffer overflow.
payload.mechanism: The vulnerable expression for image->x1 was 'image->x0 + (width - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dx + 1 + image->x0', which includes image->x0 twice. Similarly, image->y1 was 'image->y0 + (height - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dy + 1 + image->y0', including image->y0 twice. When the -d option was used to set a nonzero image_offset_x0 / image_offset_y0, the doubled offset inflated x1 and y1, leading to out-of-bounds heap writes in downstream frame processing. The fix removes the redundant '+ image->x0' and '+ image->y0' terms.

payload.pattern: image->x1 = (OPJ_UINT32)(image->x0 + (width  - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32) params->subsampling_dx + 1 + image->x0); / image->y1 = (OPJ_UINT32)(image->y0 + (height - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32) params->subsampling_dy + 1 + image->y0)

payload.description: image->x1 is computed as 'image->x0 + (width - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dx + 1 + image->x0', adding image->x0 a second time inside the parenthesized sub-expression. image->y1 is computed as 'image->y0 + (height - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dy + 1 + image->y0', adding image->y0 a second time inside the parenthesized sub-expression. These doubled offsets inflate x1/y1 when image_offset_x0/image_offset_y0 are non-zero.

The redundant '+ image->x0' term is removed from the image->x1 calculation line, and the redundant '+ image->y0' term is removed from the image->y1 calculation line, so the endpoint is computed as '(offset) + (dimension - 1) * subsampling + 1' without doubling the offset.

payload.summary: In pngtoimage(), image->x1 and image->y1 doubled image->x0/image->y0 in their computation when the -d option set a non-zero image offset, producing inflated endpoint values that caused a heap buffer overflow.
payload.mechanism: The vulnerable expression for image->x1 was 'image->x0 + (width - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dx + 1 + image->x0', which includes image->x0 twice. Similarly, image->y1 was 'image->y0 + (height - 1) * (OPJ_UINT32)params->subsampling_dy + 1 + image->y0', including image->y0 twice. When the -d option was used to set a nonzero image_offset_x0 / image_offset_y0, the doubled offset inflated x1 and y1, leading to out-of-bounds heap writes in downstream frame processing. The fix removes the redundant '+ image->x0' and '+ image->y0' terms.

payload.summary: Missing NULL check on tls1_lookup_sigalg() return value leads to NULL pointer dereference in tls1_check_sig_alg()
payload.mechanism: In tls1_check_sig_alg(), when use_pc_sigalgs is true, tls1_lookup_sigalg() is called to look up a peer-supplied signature algorithm value. If the peer sends an unsupported value, tls1_lookup_sigalg() returns NULL, but the code unconditionally dereferences sigalg->sigandhash in the comparison, causing a NULL pointer dereference that crashes the application.

obs-git-show-stat-eb563247aef3.snippet:
commit eb563247aef3e83dda7679c43f9649270462e5b1
Author: Benjamin Kaduk <kaduk@mit.edu>
Date:   Fri Apr 10 12:27:28 2020 -0700

    Fix NULL dereference in SSL_check_chain() for TLS 1.3
    
    In the tls1_check_sig_alg() helper function, we loop through the list of
    "signature_algorithms_cert" values received from the client and attempt
    to look up each one in turn in our internal table that maps wire
    codepoint to string-form name, digest and/or signature NID, etc., in
    order to compare the signature scheme from the peer's list against what
    is used to sign the certificates in the certificate chain we're
    checking.  Unfortunately, when the peer sends a value that we don't
    support, the lookup returns NULL, but we unconditionally dereference the
    lookup result for the comparison, leading to an application crash
    triggerable by an unauthenticated client.
    
    Since we will not be able to say anything about algorithms we don't
    recognize, treat NULL return from lookup as "does not match".
    
    We currently only apply the "signature_algorithm_cert" checks on TLS 1.3
    connections, so previous TLS versions are unaffected.  SSL_check_chain()
    is not called directly from libssl, but may be used by the application
    inside a callback (e.g., client_hello or cert callback) to verify that a
    candidate certificate chain will be acceptable to the client.
    
    CVE-2020-1967
    
    Reviewed-by: Matt Caswell <matt@openssl.org>

 ssl/t1_lib.c | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)

eb563247ae Fix NULL dereference in SSL_check_chain() for TLS 1.3
e7ff223a20 Update copyright year

payload.pattern: if (sig_nid == sigalg->sigandhash)

payload.description: The pointer sigalg returned by tls1_lookup_sigalg() is dereferenced via sigalg->sigandhash without a prior NULL check, allowing a NULL pointer dereference when the peer sends an unsupported signature algorithm.

A NULL guard check sigalg != NULL is added before dereferencing sigalg->sigandhash, so that an unsupported signature algorithm lookup returning NULL is treated as 'does not match' instead of causing a crash.
sigalg != NULL — short-circuits the sig_nid comparison when the signature algorithm lookup returned NULL.

payload.summary: Missing NULL check on tls1_lookup_sigalg() return value leads to NULL pointer dereference in tls1_check_sig_alg()
payload.mechanism: In tls1_check_sig_alg(), when use_pc_sigalgs is true, tls1_lookup_sigalg() is called to look up a peer-supplied signature algorithm value. If the peer sends an unsupported value, tls1_lookup_sigalg() returns NULL, but the code unconditionally dereferences sigalg->sigandhash in the comparison, causing a NULL pointer dereference that crashes the application.

payload.summary: GEN_EDIPARTY was not handled as a distinct GENERAL_NAME type; it fell through to GEN_X400 case blocks in GENERAL_NAME_cmp, GENERAL_NAME_set0_value, and GENERAL_NAME_get0_value, causing the EDIPARTYNAME structure to be accessed through the wrong union member d.other (ASN1_TYPE*) instead of d.ediPartyName (EDIPARTYNAME*), which led to ASN1_TYPE_cmp() dereferencing an invalid pointer and causing a NULL pointer dereference segmentation fault.
payload.mechanism: The switch statements in GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), GENERAL_NAME_set0_value(), and GENERAL_NAME_get0_value() all had `case GEN_X400:` immediately followed by `case GEN_EDIPARTY:` without a break, making GEN_EDIPARTY fall through and inherit the GEN_X400 handling code. This handling code accessed union member `d.other` (typed as `ASN1_TYPE*`), which is the wrong member for EDIPARTYNAME data (which should use `d.ediPartyName` typed as `EDIPARTYNAME*`). In GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), calling `ASN1_TYPE_cmp(a->d.other, b->d.other)` on EDIPARTYNAME data caused the ASN1_TYPE comparison machinery to interpret the EDIPARTYNAME structure fields as ASN1_TYPE pointers, dereferencing a value at the union offset that could be NULL or an invalid address, producing a segmentation fault (NULL pointer dereference). The fix separates GEN_EDIPARTY into its own case with a break, uses the correct union member d.ediPartyName in all three functions, and introduces a dedicated edipartyname_cmp() function with proper NULL guards for safe EDIPARTYNAME structural comparison.

commit f960d81215ebf3f65e03d4d5d857fb9b666d6920
Author:     Matt Caswell <matt@openssl.org>
AuthorDate: Wed Nov 11 16:12:58 2020 +0000
Commit:     Matt Caswell <matt@openssl.org>
CommitDate: Tue Dec 8 10:18:44 2020 +0000

    Correctly compare EdiPartyName in GENERAL_NAME_cmp()
    
    If a GENERAL_NAME field contained EdiPartyName data then it was
    incorrectly being handled as type "other". This could lead to a
    segmentation fault.
    
    Many thanks to David Benjamin from Google for r

 ASN1_SEQUENCE(OTHERNAME) = {

         /* Maybe have a true ANY DEFINED BY later */
         ASN1_EXP(OTHERNAME, value, ASN1_ANY, 0)
 } ASN1_SEQUENCE_END(OTHERNAME)
 
 IMPLEMENT_ASN1_FUNCTIONS(OTHERNAME)
 
 ASN1_SEQUENCE(EDIPARTYNAME) = {
         /* DirectoryString is a CHOICE type so use explicit tagging */
         ASN1_EXP_OPT(EDIPARTYNAME, nameAssigner, DIRECTORYSTRING, 0),
         ASN1_EXP(EDIPARTYNAME, partyName, DIRECTORYSTRING, 1)
 } ASN1_SEQUENCE_END(EDIPARTYNAME)
 
 IMPLEMENT_ASN1_FUNCTIONS(EDIPARTYNAME)
 
 ASN1_CHOICE(GENERAL_NAME) = {
         ASN1_IMP(GENERAL_NAME, d.otherName, OTHERNAME, GEN_OTHERNAME),
         ASN1_IMP(GENERAL_NAME, d.rfc822Name, ASN1_IA5STRING, GEN_EMAIL),
         ASN1_IMP(GENERAL_NAME, d.dNSName, ASN1_IA5STRING, GEN_DNS),

         ASN1_IMP(GENERAL_NAME, d.otherName, OTHERNAME, GEN_OTHERNAME),
         ASN1_IMP(GENERAL_NAME, d.rfc822Name, ASN1_IA5STRING, GEN_EMAIL),
