observation_payloads.claim: git log --follow around changed path crypto/x509v3/v3_genn.c
observation_payloads.snippet:
f960d81215 Correctly compare EdiPartyName in GENERAL_NAME_cmp()
aa0ad2011d DirectoryString is a CHOICE type and therefore uses explicit tagging
35fd99538a Update copyright year
dc9f2a69f3 issue-8973: Added const to parameters for values that were not altered
04761b557a Fix memory leak in GENERAL_NAME_set0_othername.

payload.pattern: case GEN_EDIPARTY: result = ASN1_TYPE_cmp(a->d.other, b->d.other);
case GEN_EDIPARTY: a->d.other = value;
case GEN_EDIPARTY: return a->d.other;
static int edipartyname_cmp(const EDIPARTYNAME *a, const EDIPARTYNAME *b)

payload.description: In GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case falls through to GEN_X400, causing ASN1_TYPE_cmp() to be called with a->d.other and b->d.other, which accesses the wrong union member for EDIPARTYNAME data and interprets it as ASN1_TYPE instead of EDIPARTYNAME.
In GENERAL_NAME_set0_value(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case stores the value to a->d.other instead of a->d.ediPartyName, writing EDIPARTYNAME data to the wrong union field.
In GENERAL_NAME_get0_value(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case returns a->d.other instead of a->d.ediPartyName, reading EDIPARTYNAME data from the wrong union field.

In GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case is separated from GEN_X400 with its own break, and now calls the new edipartyname_cmp(a->d.ediPartyName, b->d.ediPartyName) using the correct union member.
A new static function edipartyname_cmp() is introduced that properly compares EDIPARTYNAME structures by checking NULL pointers and comparing nameAssigner and partyName fields with ASN1_STRING_cmp.
In GENERAL_NAME_set0_value(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case now stores to a->d.ediPartyName instead of a->d.other, using the correct union member.
In GENERAL_NAME_get0_value(), the GEN_EDIPARTY case now returns a->d.ediPartyName instead of a->d.other, using the correct union member.

edipartyname_cmp() guards against NULL EDIPARTYNAME pointers (a==NULL || b==NULL) and NULL partyName fields before dereferencing, preventing the NULL pointer dereference that occurred when ASN1_TYPE_cmp was called on EDIPARTYNAME data via the wrong union member.

The vulnerable code lacked a distinct case branch for GEN_EDIPARTY in GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), GENERAL_NAME_set0_value(), and GENERAL_NAME_get0_value(); GEN_EDIPARTY unconditionally fell through to the GEN_X400 handling code, accessing the wrong union member d.other instead of d.ediPartyName.

payload.summary: GEN_EDIPARTY was not handled as a distinct GENERAL_NAME type; it fell through to GEN_X400 case blocks in GENERAL_NAME_cmp, GENERAL_NAME_set0_value, and GENERAL_NAME_get0_value, causing the EDIPARTYNAME structure to be accessed through the wrong union member d.other (ASN1_TYPE*) instead of d.ediPartyName (EDIPARTYNAME*), which led to ASN1_TYPE_cmp() dereferencing an invalid pointer and causing a NULL pointer dereference segmentation fault.
payload.mechanism: The switch statements in GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), GENERAL_NAME_set0_value(), and GENERAL_NAME_get0_value() all had `case GEN_X400:` immediately followed by `case GEN_EDIPARTY:` without a break, making GEN_EDIPARTY fall through and inherit the GEN_X400 handling code. This handling code accessed union member `d.other` (typed as `ASN1_TYPE*`), which is the wrong member for EDIPARTYNAME data (which should use `d.ediPartyName` typed as `EDIPARTYNAME*`). In GENERAL_NAME_cmp(), calling `ASN1_TYPE_cmp(a->d.other, b->d.other)` on EDIPARTYNAME data caused the ASN1_TYPE comparison machinery to interpret the EDIPARTYNAME structure fields as ASN1_TYPE pointers, dereferencing a value at the union offset that could be NULL or an invalid address, producing a segmentation fault (NULL pointer dereference). The fix separates GEN_EDIPARTY into its own case with a break, uses the correct union member d.ediPartyName in all three functions, and introduces a dedicated edipartyname_cmp() function with proper NULL guards for safe EDIPARTYNAME structural comparison.

payload.summary: EVP_CipherUpdate path lacks INT_MAX bounds check on computed output length, causing signed integer overflow in *outl for very large inputs on block ciphers.
payload.mechanism: Both evp_EncryptDecryptUpdate and EVP_DecryptUpdate compute output length as (block-aligned portion of remaining input) + (one cipher block). The result is stored in int *outl. On very large inputs (>INT_MAX bytes), the block-aligned remaining input alone can exceed INT_MAX, or the addition of the final block can cause INT_MAX wraparound on 32-bit signed int, producing a negative or incorrect output length. The commit message states 'Don't overflow the output length in EVP_CipherUpdate calls'. The fix adds explicit overflow checks against INT_MAX that reject input sizes large enough to cause this overflow.

payload.pattern: overflow guard for block-aligned output length in evp_EncryptDecryptUpdate
overflow guard for block-aligned output length in EVP_DecryptUpdate
adds #include <limits.h> to provide INT_MAX
adds EVP_R_OUTPUT_WOULD_OVERFLOW error define
adds EVP_R_OUTPUT_WOULD_OVERFLOW reason string
adds EVP_R_OUTPUT_WOULD_OVERFLOW error text entry

payload.description: In evp_EncryptDecryptUpdate, after processing j prefix bytes from input, the remaining output length is computed as ((inl - j) & ~(bl - 1)) + bl (block-aligned remainder plus one block). Prior to the fix, there is no check that this sum fits within INT_MAX, so the signed int *outl can overflow on very large inputs.

In EVP_DecryptUpdate, when final_used is set, the output length is computed as (inl & ~(b - 1)) + b (block-aligned input plus one final block). Prior to the fix, there is no check that this sum fits within INT_MAX, so the signed int *outl can overflow on very large inputs.

The fix adds a guard condition: if (((inl - j) & ~(bl - 1)) > INT_MAX - bl) then raise EVP_R_OUTPUT_WOULD_OVERFLOW and return 0. This prevents the signed int output length from overflowing by verifying that the block-aligned remainder plus one block does not exceed INT_MAX.

The fix adds a guard condition: if ((inl & ~(b - 1)) > INT_MAX - b) then raise EVP_R_OUTPUT_WOULD_OVERFLOW and return 0. This prevents the signed int output length from overflowing by verifying that the block-aligned input plus one final block does not exceed INT_MAX.
Guard: ((inl - j) & ~(bl - 1)) > INT_MAX - bl — checks that the block-aligned remaining input length does not exceed INT_MAX minus one block before proceeding with output.
Guard: (inl & ~(b - 1)) > INT_MAX - b — checks that the block-aligned input length does not exceed INT_MAX minus one block before proceeding with output.

payload.summary: EVP_CipherUpdate path lacks INT_MAX bounds check on computed output length, causing signed integer overflow in *outl for very large inputs on block ciphers.
payload.mechanism: Both evp_EncryptDecryptUpdate and EVP_DecryptUpdate compute output length as (block-aligned portion of remaining input) + (one cipher block). The result is stored in int *outl. On very large inputs (>INT_MAX bytes), the block-aligned remaining input alone can exceed INT_MAX, or the addition of the final block can cause INT_MAX wraparound on 32-bit signed int, producing a negative or incorrect output length. The commit message states 'Don't overflow the output length in EVP_CipherUpdate calls'. The fix adds explicit overflow checks against INT_MAX that reject input sizes large enough to cause this overflow.

payload.summary: Incorrect loop increment in PAuth sbox functions causes 4-bit sbox to be applied at 16-bit intervals instead of 4-bit intervals
payload.mechanism: In the PAC computation, the sbox is a 4-bit substitution box, meaning each lookup covers exactly 4 bits of the input. The loop in pac_sub and pac_inv_sub iterates over the 64-bit input with a step of b += 16, placing the 4-bit sbox lookup at bit offsets 0, 16, 32, 48 instead of 0, 4, 8, 12, ..., 60. This causes 12 out of every 16 bits of the input to bypass the sbox substitution entirely, producing incorrect PAC values.

payload.description: The sbox substitution loop iterates with step b += 16, causing the 4-bit sbox lookup sub[(i >> b) & 0xf] to be applied only at bit positions 0, 16, 32, 48 instead of every 4-bit nibble, leaving bits 4–15, 20–31, 36–47, 52–63 unsubstituted per 16-bit block.
The sbox substitution loop step is corrected to b += 4, ensuring the 4-bit sbox is applied to every consecutive 4-bit nibble of the 64-bit input at bit positions 0, 4, 8, 12, ..., 60.

payload.summary: Incorrect loop increment in PAuth sbox functions causes 4-bit sbox to be applied at 16-bit intervals instead of 4-bit intervals
payload.mechanism: In the PAC computation, the sbox is a 4-bit substitution box, meaning each lookup covers exactly 4 bits of the input. The loop in pac_sub and pac_inv_sub iterates over the 64-bit input with a step of b += 16, placing the 4-bit sbox lookup at bit offsets 0, 16, 32, 48 instead of 0, 4, 8, 12, ..., 60. This causes 12 out of every 16 bits of the input to bypass the sbox substitution entirely, producing incorrect PAC values.

payload.summary: ati_2d_blt() passes guest-controlled register values to pixman rendering functions without sufficient validation, allowing a malicious guest to trigger crashes via zero strides, zero bpp, or signed-integer coordinate wraparound.
payload.mechanism: The ATI 2D blit function ati_2d_blt() computes pixel coordinates (dst_x, dst_y, src_x, src_y), bits-per-pixel (bpp), and stride values from guest-writable hardware registers. Three classes of missing validation converge: (1) coordinate variables were declared as signed int, causing arithmetic such as s->regs.dst_x + 1 - s->regs.dst_width to underflow to negative values when a malicious guest supplies a large dst_width, sending invalid coordinates to pixman; (2) bpp can be zero when ati_bpp_from_datatype() hits its default case for an unrecognized dp_datatype, and the function proceeds without checking, causing downstream division-by-zero in pixman; (3) dst_stride and src_stride can be zero from guest-controlled pitch registers (dst_pitch, src_pitch, default_pitch), and the function proceeds without checking, causing pixman to operate on a zero-stride surface. Additionally, s->regs.dst_x and s->regs.dst_y were unconditionally incremented by dst_width/dst_height regardless of blit direction, allowing coordinate accumulation to drift out of bounds across repeated blits. The fix changes coordinate types to unsigned int, adds early-return zero-guards for bpp, dst_stride, and src_stride with LOG_GUEST_ERROR logging, and replaces unconditional accumulator increments with direction-conditional assignments.

commit ac2071c3791b67fc7af78b8ceb320c01ca1b5df7
Author: BALATON Zoltan <balaton@eik.bme.hu>
Date:   Mon Apr 6 22:34:26 2020 +0200

    ati-vga: Fix checks in ati_2d_blt() to avoid crash
    
    In some corner cases (that never happen during normal operation but a
    malicious guest could program wrong values) pixman functions were
    called with parameters that result in a crash. Fix this and add more
    checks to disallow such cases.

commit ac2071c3791b67fc7af78b8ceb320c01ca1b5df7
Author: BALATON Zoltan <balaton@eik.bme.hu>
Date:   Mon Apr 6 22:34:26 2020 +0200

    ati-vga: Fix checks in ati_2d_blt() to avoid crash
    
    In some corner cases (that never happen during normal operation but a
    malicious guest could program wrong values) pixman functions were
    called with parameters that result in a crash. Fix this and add more
    checks to disallow such cases.
    
    Reported-by: Ziming Zhang <ezrakiez@gmail.com>
    Signed-off-by: BALATON Zoltan <balaton@eik.bme.hu>
    Message-id: 20200406204029.19559747D5D@zero.eik.bme.hu
    Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>

diff --git a/hw/display/ati_2d.c b/hw/display/ati_2d.c
index 42e82311eb..23a8ae0cd8 100644
--- a/hw/display/ati_2d.c
+++ b/hw/display/ati_2d.c
@@ -1,189 +1,204 @@
 /*
  * QEMU ATI SVGA emulation
  * 2D engine functions
  *
  * Copyright (c) 2019 BALATON Zoltan
  *
  * This work is licensed under the GNU GPL license version 2 or later.
  */
 
 #include "qemu/osdep.h"
 #include "ati_int.h"
 #include "ati_regs.h"
 #include "qemu/log.h"
 #include "ui/pixel_ops.h"
 
 /*
  * NOTE:
  * This is 2D _acceleration_ and supposed to be fast. Therefore, don't try to
  * reinvent the wheel (unlikely to get better with a naive implementation than
  * existing libraries) and avoid (poorly) reimplementing gfx primitives.
  * That is unnecessary and would become a performance problem. Instead, try to
  * map to and reuse existing optimised facilities (e.g. pixman) wherever
  * possible.
  */
 
 static int ati_bpp_from_datatype(ATIVGAState *s)
 {
     switch (s->regs.dp_datatype & 0xf) {
     case 2:
         return 8;
     case 3:
     case 4:
         return 16;
     case 5:
         return 24;
     case 6:
         return 32;
     default:
         qemu_log_mask(LOG_UNIMP, "Unknown dst datatype %d\n",
                       s->regs.dp_datatype & 0xf);
         return 0;
     }
 }
 
 #define DEFAULT_CNTL (s->regs.dp_gui_master_cn

observation_payloads.snippet:
ac2071c379 ati-vga: Fix checks in ati_2d_blt() to avoid crash
584acf34cb ati-vga: Fix reverse bit blts
a38127414b ati-vga: Fix frame buffer endianness for big endian target
c799d2ee7e ati-vga: Improve readability of ati_2d_blt function
866ad5f5ff ati-vga: Fixes to offset and pitch registers

payload.pattern: int dst_x|int dst_y → unsigned dst_x|unsigned dst_y; added if(!bpp) and if(!dst_stride) zero-guards
int src_x|int src_y → unsigned src_x|unsigned src_y; added if(!src_stride) zero-guard in ROP3_SRCCOPY case
Unconditional s->regs.dst_x += dst_width and s->regs.dst_y += dst_height → direction-conditional assignment in ROP3_SRCCOPY case
Unconditional s->regs.dst_y += dst_height → direction-conditional assignment in ROP3_PATCOPY case

payload.description: Coordinate variables dst_x, dst_y, src_x, src_y declared as signed int; arithmetic expression s->regs.<coord> + 1 - s->regs.dst_width|dst_height underflows to negative values when guest-supplied dimension exceeds coordinate + 1, passing invalid coordinates to pixman.
No guard against bpp==0; ati_bpp_from_datatype() returns 0 for unrecognized dp_datatype register values (default case), and the function proceeds to pass bpp to pixman without a zero check.
No guard against dst_stride==0; dst_stride is resolved from guest-controlled dst_pitch or default_pitch registers and can be zero, causing pixman to operate on a zero-stride destination surface.
No guard against src_stride==0; src_stride is resolved from guest-controlled src_pitch or default_pitch registers and can be zero, causing pixman to operate on a zero-stride source surface.

Unconditional accumulator increments s->regs.dst_x += s->regs.dst_width and s->regs.dst_y += s->regs.dst_height execute regardless of blit direction bits (DST_X_LEFT_TO_RIGHT / DST_Y_TOP_TO_BOTTOM), causing coordinate drift out of valid surface bounds on repeated blit calls.
Coordinate variables dst_x, dst_y, src_x, src_y types changed from signed int to unsigned int, preventing negative coordinate values from reaching pixman when arithmetic underflows.
Zero-guard added for bpp: if (!bpp) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Invalid bpp\n"); return; }, preventing division-by-zero in downstream pixman operations.
Zero-guard added for dst_stride: if (!dst_stride) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Zero dest pitch\n"); return; }, preventing pixman from operating on a zero-stride destination surface.
Zero-guard added for src_stride: if (!src_stride) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Zero source pitch\n"); return; }, preventing pixman from operating on a zero-stride source surface.

Unconditional accumulator increments replaced with direction-conditional assignments: s->regs.dst_x = (dp_cntl & DST_X_LEFT_TO_RIGHT ? dst_x + dst_width : dst_x) and s->regs.dst_y = (dp_cntl & DST_Y_TOP_TO_BOTTOM ? dst_y + dst_height : dst_y), preventing coordinate drift.
if (!bpp) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Invalid bpp\n"); return; } — guards against zero bpp from unrecognized dp_datatype register values.
if (!dst_stride) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Zero dest pitch\n"); return; } — guards against zero destination stride from guest-controlled pitch registers.
if (!src_stride) { qemu_log_mask(LOG_GUEST_ERROR, "Zero source pitch\n"); return; } — guards against zero source stride from guest-controlled pitch registers.

payload.summary: ati_2d_blt() passes guest-controlled register values to pixman rendering functions without sufficient validation, allowing a malicious guest to trigger crashes via zero strides, zero bpp, or signed-integer coordinate wraparound.
payload.mechanism: The ATI 2D blit function ati_2d_blt() computes pixel coordinates (dst_x, dst_y, src_x, src_y), bits-per-pixel (bpp), and stride values from guest-writable hardware registers. Three classes of missing validation converge: (1) coordinate variables were declared as signed int, causing arithmetic such as s->regs.dst_x + 1 - s->regs.dst_width to underflow to negative values when a malicious guest supplies a large dst_width, sending invalid coordinates to pixman; (2) bpp can be zero when ati_bpp_from_datatype() hits its default case for an unrecognized dp_datatype, and the function proceeds without checking, causing downstream division-by-zero in pixman; (3) dst_stride and src_stride can be zero from guest-controlled pitch registers (dst_pitch, src_pitch, default_pitch), and the function proceeds without checking, causing pixman to operate on a zero-stride surface. Additionally, s->regs.dst_x and s->regs.dst_y were unconditionally incremented by dst_width/dst_height regardless of blit direction, allowing coordinate accumulation to drift out of bounds across repeated blits. The fix changes coordinate types to unsigned int, adds early-return zero-guards for bpp, dst_stride, and src_stride with LOG_GUEST_ERROR logging, and replaces unconditional accumulator increments with direction-conditional assignments.

payload.summary: Inverted selection logic in iscsi_aio_ioctl_cb computes copy length ss as the maximum instead of the minimum of mx_sb_len and sb_len_wr, causing memcpy to read beyond the source buffer.
payload.mechanism: The variable 'ss' controls how many bytes are copied from '&acb->task->datain.data[2]' into the sense buffer via memcpy. The original ternary expression '(acb->ioh->mx_sb_len >= acb->ioh->sb_len_wr) ? acb->ioh->mx_sb_len : acb->ioh->sb_len_wr' selects the larger of mx_sb_len and sb_len_wr. When mx_sb_len (the maximum allowed sense buffer length, e.g. 252 bytes) exceeds sb_len_wr (the actual available data length derived from datain.size - 2, e.g. 100 bytes), ss is assigned mx_sb_len. The subsequent memcpy then attempts to read 252 bytes from a source region that only holds 100 bytes, reading past the end of 'datain.data[2]' into unrelated heap memory. The fix replaces the ternary with MIN() to correctly select the smaller value, bounding the copy to the actual available data.

Author:     Chen Qun <kuhn.chenqun@huawei.com>

CommitDate: Mon Apr 20 11:31:46 2020 +0100

    block/iscsi:fix heap-buffer-overflow in iscsi_aio_ioctl_cb
    
    There is an overflow, the source 'datain.data[2]' is 100 bytes,
     but the 'ss' is 252 bytes.This may cause a security issue because
     we can access a lot of unrelated memory data.

    There is an overflow, the source 'datain.data[2]' is 100 bytes,
     but the 'ss' is 252 bytes.This may cause a security issue because
     we can access a lot of unrelated memory data.
    
    The len for sbp copy data should take the minimum of mx_sb_len and
     sb_len_wr, not the maximum.
    
    If we use iscsi device for VM backend storage, ASAN show stack:

payload.pattern: ss = (acb->ioh->mx_sb_len >= acb->ioh->sb_len_wr) ? acb->ioh->mx_sb_len : acb->ioh->sb_len_wr; replaced by ss = MIN(acb->ioh->mx_sb_len, acb->ioh->sb_len_wr);

payload.description: The variable 'ss' (memcpy length) is computed via ternary '(acb->ioh->mx_sb_len >= acb->ioh->sb_len_wr) ? acb->ioh->mx_sb_len : acb->ioh->sb_len_wr', which selects the maximum of the two values. This allows 'ss' to become mx_sb_len (e.g. 252) even when sb_len_wr (the actual source data length from datain.size - 2) is smaller (e.g. 100), causing the memcpy at line 996 to read beyond the end of the source '&acb->task->datain.data[2]'.

The variable 'ss' is now computed via 'MIN(acb->ioh->mx_sb_len, acb->ioh->sb_len_wr)', which selects the minimum of the two values. This ensures the memcpy length never exceeds the actual source data size, preventing the heap buffer over-read.

payload.summary: Inverted selection logic in iscsi_aio_ioctl_cb computes copy length ss as the maximum instead of the minimum of mx_sb_len and sb_len_wr, causing memcpy to read beyond the source buffer.
payload.mechanism: The variable 'ss' controls how many bytes are copied from '&acb->task->datain.data[2]' into the sense buffer via memcpy. The original ternary expression '(acb->ioh->mx_sb_len >= acb->ioh->sb_len_wr) ? acb->ioh->mx_sb_len : acb->ioh->sb_len_wr' selects the larger of mx_sb_len and sb_len_wr. When mx_sb_len (the maximum allowed sense buffer length, e.g. 252 bytes) exceeds sb_len_wr (the actual available data length derived from datain.size - 2, e.g. 100 bytes), ss is assigned mx_sb_len. The subsequent memcpy then attempts to read 252 bytes from a source region that only holds 100 bytes, reading past the end of 'datain.data[2]' into unrelated heap memory. The fix replaces the ternary with MIN() to correctly select the smaller value, bounding the copy to the actual available data.

payload.summary: Unbounded recursion in fAbstractSyntaxNType allows stack overflow via crafted BACnet packets
payload.mechanism: fAbstractSyntaxNType in epan/dissectors/packet-bacapp.c lacked a recursion depth bound. It calls several other functions which in turn call back into fAbstractSyntaxNType. Without a depth limit, a malformed BACnet packet with deeply nested abstract syntax constructs triggers unbounded mutual recursion, exhausting the stack. The fix introduces a per-packet recursion depth counter initialized to zero in dissect_bacapp, incremented in fAbstractSyntaxNType before the processing loop, compared against BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH (100), and decremented on return. When the limit is exceeded, the function returns early, preventing stack overflow.

payload.pattern: recursion_depth >= BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH
BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH 100
p_add_proto_data(pinfo->pool, pinfo, proto_bacapp, 0, GUINT_TO_POINTER(0))
recursion_depth--

payload.description: fAbstractSyntaxNType in epan/dissectors/packet-bacapp.c could call itself recursively (directly or through callees) without any recursion depth limit, allowing unbounded stack growth when processing malformed BACnet packets.

Added a recursion depth counter in fAbstractSyntaxNType using p_get_proto_data/p_add_proto_data with a BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH guard (100). When the counter reaches the limit, the function returns early via 'return offset' instead of continuing recursion, and the counter is decremented on each return path.
Guard condition 'if (++recursion_depth >= BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH)' in fAbstractSyntaxNType checks whether recursion depth has reached the configured maximum (100) and returns early when exceeded.

payload.summary: Unbounded recursion in fAbstractSyntaxNType allows stack overflow via crafted BACnet packets
payload.mechanism: fAbstractSyntaxNType in epan/dissectors/packet-bacapp.c lacked a recursion depth bound. It calls several other functions which in turn call back into fAbstractSyntaxNType. Without a depth limit, a malformed BACnet packet with deeply nested abstract syntax constructs triggers unbounded mutual recursion, exhausting the stack. The fix introduces a per-packet recursion depth counter initialized to zero in dissect_bacapp, incremented in fAbstractSyntaxNType before the processing loop, compared against BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH (100), and decremented on return. When the limit is exceeded, the function returns early, preventing stack overflow.

payload.summary: Unbounded recursion in nfs_full_name_snoop due to missing cycle detection and depth limit when traversing parent NFS file handles
payload.mechanism: The function nfs_full_name_snoop recursively traverses parent NFS file handles to construct a full path name. Before the fix, when a parent_nns was found via g_hash_table_lookup, the function unconditionally called itself (nfs_full_name_snoop(parent_nns, len, name, pos)) without tracking recursion depth or detecting cycles among file handles. A crafted NFS packet containing file handles that form a cyclic graph causes infinite mutual recursion, exhausting the call stack and crashing Wireshark.

e6e98eab8e NFS: Add filesystem cycle detection.
bbc327f73b NFS: Fix hash table key memory corruption
bd1cdb5f98 nfs: fix displaying of operation CLONE

payload.description: nfs_full_name_snoop calls itself recursively with parent_nns without any recursion depth tracking or cycle detection, allowing unbounded recursion when parent file handles form a cycle

nfs_full_name_snoop now tracks recursion depth via p_get_proto_data/p_add_proto_data, increments fs_depth on entry, checks ++fs_depth >= NFS_MAX_FS_DEPTH, sets nns->fs_cycle=TRUE and returns early when the limit is reached, and decrements fs_depth on exit
++fs_depth >= NFS_MAX_FS_DEPTH bounds recursion depth in nfs_full_name_snoop to NFS_MAX_FS_DEPTH (100)

payload.summary: Unbounded recursion in nfs_full_name_snoop due to missing cycle detection and depth limit when traversing parent NFS file handles
payload.mechanism: The function nfs_full_name_snoop recursively traverses parent NFS file handles to construct a full path name. Before the fix, when a parent_nns was found via g_hash_table_lookup, the function unconditionally called itself (nfs_full_name_snoop(parent_nns, len, name, pos)) without tracking recursion depth or detecting cycles among file handles. A crafted NFS packet containing file handles that form a cyclic graph causes infinite mutual recursion, exhausting the call stack and crashing Wireshark.

payload.summary: In dissect_eventdata_cmd, the entire offset-advancing block was gated behind `if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)`. When the protocol tree is NULL (e.g., dissection without tree construction), offset never advances, causing the calling for-loop in dissect_event_cmd to loop infinitely.
payload.mechanism: The function dissect_eventdata_cmd parses event data from a tvbuff, advancing `offset` through fields. In the vulnerable code, the entire field-parsing and offset-advancing block was wrapped inside `if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)`. When `gvcp_telegram_tree` is NULL — which happens when Wireshark performs dissection without building a protocol tree (e.g., during a first pass or when the tree is not requested) — the entire block is skipped. The `offset` variable is never incremented. The caller, dissect_event_cmd, calls dissect_eventdata_cmd inside a `for (i = 0; i < event_count; i++)` loop that depends on offset advancing to eventually consume the packet data and terminate. With offset stuck at its entry value, the loop condition never becomes false, resulting in an infinite loop (CWE-835). The fix removes the `if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)` guard so that offset always advances regardless of tree state. The proto_tree_add_item calls are safe with a NULL tree — they simply become no-ops.

payload.pattern: if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)

payload.description: All offset-advancing logic within dissect_eventdata_cmd is conditionally executed only when `gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL`. When the protocol tree is NULL, the `offset` variable is never incremented despite the function being called iteratively from a caller loop.

The `if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)` guard is removed. The field-parsing and offset-advancing block now executes unconditionally, ensuring `offset` always advances regardless of whether the protocol tree is being built.
The condition `if (gvcp_telegram_tree != NULL)` guards all offset-advancing logic. Field parsing and offset increments are only performed when a valid protocol tree exists.

When `gvcp_telegram_tree == NULL` (protocol tree is not being constructed, e.g., during first-pass dissection or when tree output is disabled), the entire offset-advancing block is skipped, leaving `offset` unchanged while the caller loop continues to iterate.
